class_schedule.topic: Ethical Frameworks and the PMI Code of Ethics
learning_objectives: Articulate the four values of the PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct
Apply utilitarian, deontological, and virtue ethics frameworks to PM decisions
Identify ethical dilemmas unique to the project management profession
readings: PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct (full document)

lecture_notes.introduction: Project managers face ethical dilemmas regularly: pressure to underreport risks, conflicts of interest, resource misuse. The PMI Code provides a framework for navigating these situations.
key_concepts: PMI Code values: responsibility, respect, fairness, honesty
Aspirational standards (what we strive for) vs. mandatory standards (enforceable requirements)
Utilitarian ethics: greatest good for the greatest number
Deontological ethics: duty-based, following rules regardless of consequences
Virtue ethics: what would a person of good character do?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing three PM ethical dilemmas using all three ethical frameworks and comparing conclusions
setup_requirements: PMI Code of Ethics reference card

review.value: Mandatory: do not engage in or condone behavior designed to deceive others; report truthfully, even when the truth is uncomfortable
Different frameworks can reach different conclusions; ethical reasoning requires considering multiple perspectives

class_schedule.topic: Conflicts of Interest and Professional Responsibility
learning_objectives: Identify and manage conflicts of interest in project environments
Navigate dual loyalties between organization and profession
Apply whistleblowing considerations when encountering wrongdoing
readings: PMI Code of Ethics (responsibility and fairness sections)
Johnson, Ethics in Engineering, Ch. 5 (conflicts of interest)

lecture_notes.introduction: Conflicts of interest are not always obvious. The PM must be vigilant about situations where personal interests could compromise professional judgment.
key_concepts: Conflict of interest: personal interest that could improperly influence professional judgment
Common PM conflicts: vendor relationships, hiring decisions, resource allocation favoring allies
Disclosure obligation: proactively disclose potential conflicts to stakeholders
Dual loyalty: when organizational pressure conflicts with professional ethical obligations
Whistleblowing: reporting wrongdoing, legal protections, retaliation risks, reporting channels
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study analysis of a PM who discovers their organization is misrepresenting project status to a client

review.value: The ethical obligation is to disclose the conflict, not necessarily to withdraw; disclosure allows stakeholders to decide
Ethical obligation to report wrongdoing; start with internal channels; external reporting when internal channels fail or are complicit

class_schedule.topic: Ethical Issues in Estimation, Reporting, and Risk Disclosure
learning_objectives: Identify ethical pitfalls in project estimation and reporting
Navigate pressure to underreport risks or overstate progress
Apply honesty standards to status reporting and earned value data
readings: Flyvbjerg, Megaprojects and Risk, Ch. 2 (strategic misrepresentation)

lecture_notes.introduction: The temptation to shade the truth in project reporting is enormous. Organizational pressure to deliver good news can corrupt the information flow that governance depends on.
key_concepts: Strategic misrepresentation: deliberately underestimating costs or overestimating benefits to get project approval
Optimism bias vs. deliberate deception: distinguishing honest mistakes from ethical violations
Watermelon projects: green on the outside (status reports), red on the inside (actual state)
Ethical reporting: reporting what you know, what you don't know, and what you suspect
Creating safe environments: making it safe for team members to report bad news
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a real-world case of a major project that concealed problems until catastrophic failure
setup_requirements: Case study materials (e.g., FBI Virtual Case File project)

review.value: Flyvbjerg: promoters deliberately underestimate costs to secure approval; this is not optimism, it is deception
If reporting bad news gets people punished, bad news will be hidden until it is too late to fix

class_schedule.topic: Ethical Leadership, Diversity, and Inclusion in Projects
learning_objectives: Model ethical leadership behaviors in project environments
Address bias and promote equity in team management
Navigate ethical challenges in global and diverse team environments
readings: PMI Code of Ethics (respect and fairness sections)

lecture_notes.introduction: Ethical leadership goes beyond personal compliance. It means creating environments where ethical behavior is the norm and unethical behavior is not tolerated.
key_concepts: Ethical leadership: setting the tone, modeling behavior, holding others accountable
Implicit bias: unconscious preferences that affect hiring, assignment, evaluation decisions
Equity vs. equality: providing what each person needs vs. treating everyone the same
Cultural ethical relativism vs. universalism: when ethical standards differ across cultures
Inclusive project management: ensuring diverse perspectives are heard and valued
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing hiring and assignment decisions for implicit bias using structured decision criteria

review.value: If the PM tolerates unethical behavior, the team will emulate it; ethical standards are demonstrated, not just documented
Gift-giving is normal in some cultures and bribery in others; understanding the context matters, but anti-corruption laws apply regardless

class_schedule.topic: Ethics in Technology, AI, and Sustainability Projects
learning_objectives: Evaluate ethical implications of technology projects including AI and data
Apply sustainability ethics to project decision-making
Navigate the tension between project objectives and broader societal impact

lecture_notes.introduction: Technology projects create ethical dilemmas that did not exist a generation ago. Project managers must consider the societal impact of what they build, not just whether it meets requirements.
key_concepts: AI ethics: bias in algorithms, explainability, accountability for automated decisions
Data ethics: privacy, consent, surveillance, data ownership
Sustainability ethics: environmental impact of projects, future generation considerations
Stakeholder capitalism: balancing shareholder returns with societal impact
Ethical impact assessment: proactively evaluating the ethical implications of project deliverables
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting an ethical impact assessment for an AI-powered hiring tool project

review.value: AI systems trained on biased data produce biased outcomes; the PM has a responsibility to ensure fairness in the systems they build
Proactive evaluation of who could be harmed by the project deliverables; should be done during planning, not after deployment

learning_objectives: Design organizational mechanisms that support ethical project management
Prepare for ethical challenges in professional PM practice
readings: Gentile, Giving Voice to Values, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Ethics is not a topic for one course. It is a practice for an entire career. Building ethical habits and organizational cultures ensures long-term professional integrity.
key_concepts: Ethical culture elements: clear standards, leadership modeling, reporting mechanisms, consequences, recognition
Ethics training: scenario-based, ongoing, role-specific
Reporting mechanisms: anonymous hotlines, ombudsman, open-door policies
Personal ethical framework: a decision-making process for when the answer is not clear
Giving Voice to Values: practicing ethical responses before you need them
lecture_notes.demonstration: Developing a personal ethical decision-making framework and practicing it on realistic scenarios

review.value: Rehearse ethical responses in advance; knowing what to say and how to say it makes it more likely you'll act ethically under pressure
Culture eats policy for breakfast; documented ethics policies mean nothing if the culture rewards cutting corners

quizzes.topic: Ethical Frameworks and Conflicts of Interest

questions.question: A PM's brother-in-law owns a consulting firm that has submitted a proposal for the project. The PM is on the vendor selection committee. Using the PMI Code of Ethics, explain what the PM must do and why.
questions.answer: The PM must immediately disclose the family relationship to the selection committee chair and project sponsor. Per the PMI Code (Fairness, mandatory standard 5.3.2): 'We proactively and fully disclose any real or potential conflicts of interest to the appropriate stakeholders.' The PM should recuse themselves from evaluating the brother-in-law's proposal and ideally from the entire selection process to avoid even the appearance of impropriety. If the organization decides the PM can remain on the committee for other vendors, the recusal and disclosure must be documented in writing. The PM should NOT simply remain silent and hope they can be objective; the duty is disclosure, regardless of intent.

rubric.key: Identifies the conflict of interest correctly
Recommends disclosure and recusal with documentation

questions.question: Apply utilitarian and deontological ethical frameworks to this dilemma: A PM knows that reporting the project's true status (6 weeks behind) will cause the sponsor to cancel the project, resulting in 15 team members losing their jobs. Padding the report could buy time for the team to catch up. What should the PM do?
questions.answer: Utilitarian analysis: padding the report could save 15 jobs IF the team catches up, but if they don't, the delay grows worse and the eventual cancellation is more costly, affecting more people. The honest report enables the sponsor to make an informed decision, possibly finding alternatives (scope reduction, deadline extension) that save both the project and jobs. Greatest good: report honestly. Deontological analysis: the PM has a duty to report truthfully per the PMI Code (Honesty, mandatory standard 3.3.1: 'We do not engage in or condone behavior that is designed to deceive others'). Padding the report is deception regardless of intent. Duty-based: report honestly. Both frameworks reach the same conclusion: report the truth. The PM should present the status along with recovery options and recommendations, giving the sponsor the information needed to make a good decision.

rubric.key: Recommends honest reporting with recovery options

quizzes.topic: Ethical Reporting, Leadership, and Technology Ethics

questions.question: What is the difference between optimism bias and strategic misrepresentation in project estimation, and why does the distinction matter ethically?
questions.answer: Optimism bias is an unconscious cognitive tendency to underestimate costs and durations while overestimating benefits. It is a psychological phenomenon, not an ethical violation, because the estimator genuinely believes their optimistic estimates. Strategic misrepresentation is the deliberate manipulation of estimates to secure project approval, knowing the estimates are unrealistically low. This IS an ethical violation because it involves intentional deception. The distinction matters because: the remedy for optimism bias is process improvement (reference class forecasting, independent estimates, contingency reserves), while the remedy for strategic misrepresentation is ethical accountability (disclosure requirements, independent cost reviews, consequences for deception). Treating strategic misrepresentation as merely optimism bias allows willful dishonesty to go unchecked.

rubric.key: Explains why the distinction matters ethically

questions.question: Your project is building a facial recognition system for a retail client. What ethical considerations should the PM raise, and at what project phase?
questions.answer: Ethical considerations the PM should raise during the PLANNING phase (before significant investment): (1) Privacy: facial recognition collects biometric data without explicit consent from shoppers; may violate GDPR, BIPA, or local privacy laws. (2) Bias: facial recognition systems have documented higher error rates for people with darker skin tones; deploying a biased system creates discrimination risk. (3) Surveillance: customers may not know they are being tracked; raises civil liberties concerns. (4) Data security: biometric data is irrevocable if breached (unlike passwords, faces cannot be changed). (5) Regulatory risk: several jurisdictions have banned or restricted facial recognition. The PM should conduct an ethical impact assessment during planning and present findings to the sponsor with recommendations. Waiting until execution or deployment is too late.

rubric.key: Identifies at least 3 specific ethical concerns
Specifies planning phase as the right time
Recommends ethical impact assessment with sponsor engagement

major_assignments.title: Ethical Analysis Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Analyze five ethical dilemmas in project management, applying multiple ethical frameworks, PMI Code of Ethics, and personal judgment. Include one dilemma involving technology ethics.
objectives: Apply utilitarian, deontological, and virtue ethics to PM dilemmas
Reference specific PMI Code of Ethics provisions in analysis
Develop and apply a personal ethical decision-making framework
Address ethical implications of technology in at least one scenario
deliverables: Five ethical case analyses (2-3 pages each) covering conflicts of interest, reporting honesty, resource allocation, stakeholder treatment, and technology ethics
Personal ethical decision-making framework document (2 pages)
Reflection paper on ethical challenges most likely to be faced in your career (2 pages)

criteria: Each case analyzed using at least two ethical frameworks
PMI Code of Ethics specifically referenced where applicable
Analysis demonstrates nuanced thinking, not just rule application
Includes a step for seeking input from others
Addresses what to do when frameworks conflict
Demonstrates self-awareness about personal ethical vulnerabilities
Proposes strategies for maintaining ethical standards under pressure
Rigorous ethical analysis across five cases

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on ethical frameworks, PMI Code, and ethical dilemmas

ethics_portfolio.description: Ethical analysis portfolio with five case analyses

midterm_examination.description: Ethical frameworks, conflicts of interest, and reporting ethics

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all PM ethics topics including technology

class_participation.description: Active participation in ethical debates, case discussions, and role-plays

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Ethical debate sessions require prepared participation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: Ethical debates are collaborative. The ethics portfolio is individual work reflecting personal ethical reasoning.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Ironic but essential: academic dishonesty in an ethics course results in automatic course failure and academic integrity board referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: No specialized software required. Word processor for case analyses and reflection papers.
